title: Pain aux raisins
subtitle: Laminated yeasted spirals filled with pastry cream and raisins
excerpt: Pain aux raisins are laminated pastry spirals, made from the same enriched dough as croissants, filled with vanilla pastry cream and soaked raisins, then sliced and proved before baking. The lamination gives the pastry its flakiness; the cream filling keeps the inside soft.
sourceNotes: Pain aux raisins is a classic Viennese-style laminated pastry, established in French patisserie tradition by the nineteenth century. The technique of laminating a yeasted dough with butter to create a flaky, layered crumb is now widely published and in common home-baking practice.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled spirals. Reheat at 180 degrees C (conventional) for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shaped, unbaked spirals can be refrigerated overnight on the tray and baked the next morning. They will prove slowly in the fridge.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 180 degrees C; rotate the tray at the 15-minute mark for even colour

glossaryTerms.definition: Pastry cream (creme patissiere) is a thick, set custard made from milk, egg yolks, sugar, and a starch such as plain flour or cornflour, cooked over medium heat until it thickens. In a pain aux raisins it is spread cold over the laminated dough before rolling; the cream bakes inside the spiral, keeping the interior soft and providing a vanilla richness that contrasts with the flaky layers outside.

A laminated yeasted dough is made by enclosing a cold butter block inside an enriched dough and rolling and folding the two together in a series of turns. Each fold multiplies the layers; three letter folds produce 27 distinct layers of dough and butter. During baking, water in the butter turns to steam and forces the layers apart, creating the characteristic flakiness of croissants and pain aux raisins.

content.text: Pain aux raisins is a two-day project. The dough is mixed and given a short first rise on day one, then laminated and retarded overnight in the fridge. On day two the laminated dough is filled with
and raisins, rolled into a log, sliced into spirals, proved, and baked. The result of the
is visible in the cross-section of each spiral: distinct pale layers separated by thin ribbons of darker, caramelised crust.

items.prepNote: soaked in warm water or orange juice for 20 minutes, then drained

content.text: Day one: make the dough, pastry cream, and laminate

Mix the flour, sugar, salt, and yeast in a large bowl. Add the warm milk and eggs and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Add the softened 40 g of butter and knead in for another 3 minutes. Shape into a rectangle, wrap in cling film, and chill for 30 minutes. Meanwhile, make the pastry cream: heat the milk in a small saucepan until just simmering. Whisk the yolks, sugar, and flour together in a bowl until pale. Pour the hot milk over slowly, whisking, then return to the pan and cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until very thick, about 3 minutes. Remove from the heat, stir in the vanilla, press cling film directly onto the surface, and refrigerate.

Beat the cold lamination butter between two sheets of cling film into a 20 cm square, about 1 cm thick. Remove the dough from the fridge and roll into a 40 cm square. Place the butter block in the centre, fold the dough over the butter to enclose it completely like an envelope, and press the edges to seal. Roll out to a long rectangle (60 cm by 20 cm), then fold into thirds like a letter. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Repeat the rolling and folding twice more (three folds total), resting 30 minutes in the fridge between each. After the final fold, refrigerate overnight.
Day two: fill, shape, prove, and bake

Remove the dough from the fridge. Roll out to a 40 cm by 30 cm rectangle. Spread the cold pastry cream evenly over the surface to within 2 cm of one long edge. Scatter the drained raisins over the cream. Roll up tightly from the filled long edge towards the clean edge, pressing to seal. Cut into 3 cm rounds with a sharp knife; you should get 10 to 12 spirals. Place cut-side up on lined baking trays, spacing well apart. Cover and prove at room temperature for 1.5 to 2 hours until noticeably puffed. Preheat the oven to 200 degrees C (conventional). Brush each spiral with beaten egg and bake for 20 to 25 minutes, until deep golden. Cool on a rack.
